sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgical resection (CRS + HIPEC) is a primary, potentially curative treatment modality for selected patients with peritoneal mesothelioma. It is recommended for medically operable patients with epithelioid histology and unicavitary disease where complete cytoreduction is achievable.

Principles:
- Surgical resection should be performed by surgical oncologists with experience in managing peritoneal mesothelioma [PEM-C].
- The goal of CRS is macroscopic complete resection (CC-0: no residual tumor). Near-complete cytoreduction (CC-1: residual tumor <2.5 mm) is also acceptable for epithelioid mesothelioma, as studies suggest minimal impact on 5-year OS compared to CC-0 [2].
- If macroscopic complete resection or near-complete cytoreduction is not possible, surgery should be aborted. Palliative surgery and/or HIPEC can be considered only if associated with minimal morbidity [3, PEM-C].
- Complete cytoreduction frequently requires total parietal peritonectomy, including visceral resections when necessary [4].
- Decisions are highly dependent on accurate histology. Peritoneal biopsy for diagnosis must provide enough tissue for differentiation between entities like peritoneal inclusion cyst, WDPMT, and diffuse mesothelioma subtypes. Cytology is not adequate [PEM-C].

Resectability Criteria:
Resectability is defined by the ability to achieve a macroscopic complete resection (CC-0) or near-complete resection (CC-1) with acceptable morbidity. Contraindications to surgery include: medical inoperability, the presence of ANY high-risk feature (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden with PCI >17 or CC score >1) unless surgery is reconsidered after neoadjuvant therapy [PEM-2, PEM-C].

Pre-operative Optimisation:
Patients should undergo thorough multidisciplinary evaluation. If a bevacizumab-containing regimen is administered preoperatively, there should be at least a 6-week interval between the last dose and CRS to minimize surgical complications [7, 8, PEM-C]. For patients with high-risk features (e.g., biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, high PCI), neoadjuvant systemic therapy is strongly encouraged before re-evaluation for CRS+HIPEC [PEM-2].

cards.name: Cytoreductive Surgery (CRS) + Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (HIPEC)
cards.detail:
approach: Typically involves midline laparotomy with peritonectomy procedures and visceral resections to achieve CC-0 or CC-1. Followed by intraoperative administration of heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy.
indications: Medically operable patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (predominantly epithelioid histology) where complete cytoreduction is achievable. May be considered in select cases of low-volume biphasic disease or bicavitary disease with minimal thoracic burden.
key_complications: Major morbidity reported in approximately 26-39% of patients. Acute kidney injury occurred in 35.7% in one series. Perioperative mortality reported at 1-2% in high-volume centers [14, 77].
operative_mortality: Reported as 1-2% (9/401 patients in one large study) [76].
